Document Title: Deep Learning-based Echocardiography Enhancement: Reducing Speckle Noise and Improving Temporal Resolution
Authors: G. Carcedo-Rodríguez; B. Vazquez; J. Perez-Gonzalez; N. Hevia-Montiel
Author Affiliations: Master Program in Computer Science and Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mérida, Yucatán, México; Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas, Unidad Académica en el Estado de Yucatán, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mérida, Yucatán, México; Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas, Unidad Académica en el Estado de Yucatán, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mérida, Yucatán, México; Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas, Unidad Académica en el Estado de Yucatán, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mérida, Yucatán, México
Publication Title: 2025 21st International Symposium on Biomedical Image Processing and Analysis (SIPAIM)
Abstract: Ultrasound is widely used in medical imaging due to its accessibility and cost-effectiveness. This paper presents a Deep Learning-based approach for enhancing echocardiographic imaging by addressing two challenges: speckle noise reduction and temporal resolution improvement. For denoising task, we propose the SE-U-Net model, while for temporal enhancement we employ Real-Time Intermediate Flow Estimation for frame interpolation. Multiple datasets were used for training, validation, and testing of both models. The proposed pipeline was applied in high heart rate murine models to enhance relevant information. In final evaluation, the denoising model achieved SSIM=0.99 and PSNR=38.27dB, indicating effective contrast enhancement, preservation of homogeneous regions, and reduction of small artifacts. The interpolation model, on the other hand, achieved SSIM=0.87 and PSNR=33.23dB, enabling the increase of ultrasound temporal resolution from 37 to 296 frames per second without losing image quality.

Document Title: Deep learning for Chagas disease: YOLOv8-based automatic detection of amastigote nests
Authors: D. Sosa; B. Vázquez; N. Hevia-Montiel
Author Affiliations: Ingeniería en Robótica Computacional, Universidad Politécnica de Yucatán, Mérida, Yucatán, Mexico; Unidad Académica del Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas en el Estado de Yucatán, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mérida, Yucatán, Mexico; Unidad Académica del Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas en el Estado de Yucatán, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mérida, Yucatán, Mexico
Publication Title: 2025 21st International Symposium on Biomedical Image Processing and Analysis (SIPAIM)
Abstract: Chagas disease, caused by the Trypanosoma cruzi parasite, remains a global public health problem, with cases reported even outside its traditional endemic areas. Its primary clinical manifestation occurs at the cardiac level, characterized by the formation of amastigote nests. This work presents a deep learning algorithm for automatic detection of amastigote nests in cardiac histopathological images obtained from a murine model. For the nests’ detection, a YOLOv8-based architecture was trained on histological images performing a 5-fold cross-validation, while the final tests were carried out with data unseen by the YOLOv8 from four image groups of different post-infection days. In training stage, YOLOv8 achieved 97.9% in accuracy, 98.6±0.3% in precision, and 97.2±0.8% in recall, while in the final test, the best result identified was 78.6% in accuracy, 76.4% in precision, and 81.1% in recall on images at 100× magnification. These metrics reveal the model’s ability to generalize well and its high sensitivity to unseen data. The results of this study show that the proposed algorithm is capable of identifying amastigote nests, which may be useful in clinical areas to understand the damage caused by Trypanosoma cruzi infection.
Author Keywords: Chagas disease;YOLOv8;deep learning;computer vision;object detection;biomedical imaging

Document Title: Ownership Authentication and Clinical Data Backup in DICOM Images via Robust Watermarking and Binary Data Hiding
Authors: A. A. Lopez-Martinez; E. Fragoso-Navarro; M. Cedillo-Hernandez; F. Garcia-Ugalde
Author Affiliations: SEPI ESIME Culhuacan, Instituto Politecnico Nacional, CDMX, Mexico; SEPI ESIME Culhuacan, Instituto Politecnico Nacional, CDMX, Mexico; SEPI ESIME Culhuacan, Instituto Politecnico Nacional, CDMX, Mexico; Universidad Nacional Autonoma de Mexico, CDMX, Mexico
Publication Title: 2025 10th International Conference on Frontiers of Signal Processing (ICFSP)
Abstract: Medical information is very important and delicate due to its relevance in clinical diagnosis, treatments, research and other commercial and non-commercial applications of governmental and private organizations. Recent advances in information and communication technologies have transformed the paradigm of medical data management, but also introduced new risks related to unauthorized access, manipulation, and distribution. To solve part of these problems, this paper proposes a method that employs two information security levels based on robust watermarking and binary data hiding, respectively. Firstly, clinical data is embedded into a logo using binary data hiding. Then, this logo is inserted into the spatial domain of a medical image through block segmentation, statistical information, and convolutional encoding for ownership authentication. Experimental results demonstrate logo robustness against JPEG DICOM compression in conjunction with aggressive attacks like cropping and copy-move, using bit error rate as a metric. The imperceptibility has been measured via peak signal-to-noise ratio and structural similarity index metrics.
Author Keywords: Robust watermarking;Binary data hiding;DICOM imaging;Information security;Ownership authentication;Convolutional encoding
IEEE Terms: Measurement;Image coding;Bit error rate;Authentication;Transform coding;Information security;Watermarking;Robustness;DICOM;Medical diagnostic imaging

Document Title: Causal Behaviour Modelling for Player Action Prediction in Rugby League Using Graph Neural Networks
Authors: J. Wang; R. Wang; G. Gendron; S. Heidari; D. A. Soriano Valdez; G. A. Z. Cruz; S. Hallett; M. Rogers; A. Woodward; A. G. Strozzi; R. Mitchel; M. Zhang; M. Witbrock; P. Delmas
Author Affiliations: Intelligent Vision Systems Lab (IVSLab), The University of Auckland, Auckland, New Zealand; Intelligent Vision Systems Lab (IVSLab), The University of Auckland, Auckland, New Zealand; NAOInstitute, The University of Auckland, Auckland, New Zealand; Intelligent Vision Systems Lab (IVSLab), The University of Auckland, Auckland, New Zealand; Intelligent Vision Systems Lab (IVSLab), The University of Auckland, Auckland, New Zealand; Intelligent Vision Systems Lab (IVSLab), The University of Auckland, Auckland, New Zealand; Intelligent Vision Systems Lab (IVSLab), The University of Auckland, Auckland, New Zealand; Intelligent Vision Systems Lab (IVSLab), The University of Auckland, Auckland, New Zealand; Intelligent Vision Systems Lab (IVSLab), The University of Auckland, Auckland, New Zealand; ICAT (UNAM), CDMX, Mexico; Riki Consulting, Auckland, New Zealand; Centre for Data Science and Artificial Intelligence, University of Wellington, Wellington, New Zealand; NAOInstitute, The University of Auckland, Auckland, New Zealand; Intelligent Vision Systems Lab (IVSLab), The University of Auckland, Auckland, New Zealand
Publication Title: 2024 39th International Conference on Image and Vision Computing New Zealand (IVCNZ)
Abstract: Rugby League is a dynamic full-contact team sport where two teams of 13 players compete to score points through various tactical plays. It requires advanced strategies from both teams. To analyse and predict these actions, we investigated a causal behaviour modelling approach using graph neural networks (GNNs). We established causal links between player actions using the PCMCI algorithm and used this with a GNN architecture to predict future actions. Our model was trained and evaluated on a dataset of 211 short video clips, capturing over 71,000 player actions. Our investigation showed that integrating causal modelling with GNNs can provide the best of both worlds in terms of explainability and performance, as it was competitive with traditional black-box deep learning models like LSTMs and Transformers, achieving an Fl-Score of about 90.077%. This research highlights the potential of using causality-driven A.I. frameworks for explainable sports analytics and action prediction in the fast-paced and strategic context of Rugby League.
Author Keywords: Causal Behavior Modelling;Graph Neural Networks;Rugby League;Player Action Prediction;Sports Analytics;Sequential Data Prediction
IEEE Terms: Deep learning;Analytical models;Closed box;Predictive models;Transformers;Brain modeling;Prediction algorithms;Graph neural networks;Long short term memory;Sports
